findings.title: SSH private key is not passphrase-protected
findings.explanation: The SSH private key at /home/vietanhdev/.ssh/genpc2_ed25519 (format: openssh) has no passphrase, so it is a plaintext credential on disk. Anyone who obtains a copy of that one file — from a backup, a lost or stolen laptop, a dotfiles repo synced to the cloud, or any process running as you — can authenticate as you to every server that trusts it, immediately and with nothing else required. A passphrase does not stop a determined attacker forever, but it turns "copy one file and log in" into "also crack a passphrase," and it is what lets you notice and rotate the key before it is used.
findings.fix_hint: Add a passphrase to the existing key in place (this keeps the same key, so you do not have to re-add it to any server): run 'ssh-keygen -p -f /home/vietanhdev/.ssh/genpc2_ed25519' and enter a strong passphrase when prompted. To avoid typing it on every use, load the key into your agent once per session with 'ssh-add /home/vietanhdev/.ssh/genpc2_ed25519'. If this key is shared, checked into a repo, or you are unsure who has copied it, treat it as compromised: generate a fresh one with 'ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -a 100', deploy the new public key, and remove the old key from every server's authorized_keys.
